title: OpenAI Codex Tech Lead On How His Career Grew And How He Uses Codex | Michael Bolin
guest_role: OpenAI Codex CLI tech lead, ex-Meta Distinguished Engineer (E9)

sections.title: Building a virtual filesystem for Facebook
Why Codex's harness is open source
Why deep technical skills are still valuable (for now)
Advice on writing better and career planning

key_takeaways: Bolin's three-step career algorithm: figure out what you genuinely love doing, figure out what is invaluable to your employer, and lean hard into the intersection; he left Google after four years because his passion projects (Google Tasks, Closure tools, a book on Closure) weren't what Google valued.

He built the Buck build system as a hackathon project against near-universal advice, with an existing internal competitor and Google's Blaze looming; conviction came from an 'existence proof' (he'd seen Blaze work) plus first-principles insight that caching unchanged build steps and making modules cheap to add would yield multi-x speedups.

His influence tactic for starting disruptive projects without credibility: deliberately scope-couch them ('just an Android build system', 'just an iOS editor'), borrow credibility from a respected supporter, and let adoption come voluntarily — the iOS team asked for Buck a year later, and Uber and Airbnb adopted it externally.

After his E8 promo he failed at a 'hero quest' fixing facebook.com web speed; the lesson was that he succeeds on projects that involve writing lots of code from scratch, not data-analysis and alignment-heavy work, and that senior managers deserve more recognition for pairing senior ICs with the right projects.

His E9 Distinguished promo was delayed after he pushed too aggressively when Microsoft acquired GitHub, correctly predicting Nuclide's Atom foundation would lose to VS Code; the coaching lesson was about how he pushed (recognize triggers, don't act in the moment, go through managers), not what he argued.

The Eden virtual filesystem lazily materializes files in Meta's monorepo, and his Myles file-search service used parallel arrays and a 64-bit character bitmask for cache-friendly fuzzy search over a million-plus files in 10-20ms, running on about 30 servers globally by the time he left.

He joined OpenAI because building LLM dev tools at Meta on Llama 2 couldn't compete with GPT-4, and he wanted to co-develop product with the researchers building the best model; in a research-led culture, 'if the model weren't very good, it wouldn't really matter what we did on the harness.'

Codex CLI launched rushed in April 2025; Codex Web was ahead of its time because local agents had stronger product-market fit; the August 2025 confluence of GPT-5, a new TUI, open-weights model support, and the VS Code extension created the inflection to 1M+ users and 5x usage growth, though he believes most agent compute will ultimately move to the cloud.

80-90% of his code is now model-written; he still hand-writes the security-critical sandboxing code in the Rust harness, and the CLI is open source primarily so people can inspect an agent that runs on their machine.

Education advice: deep technical skills still matter because your questions bound the agent's output quality; he recommends reading an operating systems textbook cover to cover, the O'Reilly Rust books, and CTF competitions as a 'computer decathlon' that forces breadth; his advice to his younger self is to not cling to your first programming language.

notable_quotes.speaker: Michael Bolin
notable_quotes.quote: A lot of people told me what I was doing was a very bad idea. Almost everybody, except one person.

notable_quotes.speaker: Michael Bolin
notable_quotes.quote: Facebook was always hitting the scaling limits of every mobile developer tool before everybody else... we got to solve problems that nobody had solved before, and not just as a science project, but because it was real business value.

notable_quotes.speaker: Michael Bolin
notable_quotes.quote: If the model weren't very good, it wouldn't really matter what we did on the harness.

notable_quotes.speaker: Michael Bolin
notable_quotes.quote: It's kind of amazing and sad or weird how far many of us can get in software engineering without really having a clue how computers work.
